title: Measurement-Device-Independent Entanglement Quantification in a Fully Connected Time-Bin Quantum Network
authors: Lu Liu, Ling-Xuan Kong, Ze-Yang Lu, Xu-Jie Peng, Xiao-Xu Fang, He Lu
abstract: Fully connected quantum networks enable scalable quantum communication, yet reliable entanglement characterization without trusting measurement devices remains challenging. Here we experimentally demonstrate measurement-device-independent (MDI) entanglement verification and quantification in a time-bin-encoded fully connected quantum network. Using a broadband periodically poled lithium niobate on insulator source combined with dense wavelength-division multiplexing, we distribute all six pairwise entangled links among four users over 20-km fiber channels, preserving high-fidelity entanglement without active stabilization of the long-distance fiber links. We show that conventional entanglement witnesses can fail under untrusted measurement conditions. By encoding trusted input states in the polarization degree of freedom of the same photons, we realize MDI measurements without ancillary photons or additional experimental resources. Both entanglement verification and quantification are obtained from the same measurement dataset. Our results establish a practical and scalable approach for reliable entanglement characterization in quantum networks.
summary: This paper demonstrates a way to verify and measure entanglement in a multi-user quantum network without needing to trust the measurement hardware. By using polarization to encode trusted information, the researchers successfully protected the network against time-shift attacks across 20 km of fiber.
main_problem: Reliable entanglement characterization in large-scale quantum networks is difficult because conventional entanglement witnesses are vulnerable to measurement-device attacks, such as time-shift attacks, which can falsely certify entanglement.
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrated a measurement-device-independent (MDI) protocol for entanglement verification and quantification in a four-user, six-link fully connected time-bin quantum network, maintaining high fidelity over 20-km fiber channels.
method: The researchers used a broadband PPLNOI waveguide source with wavelength-division multiplexing to distribute entangled photons and implemented an MDI approach by encoding trusted input states in the polarization degree of freedom to eliminate reliance on measurement device characterization.
model_or_system: A four-user fully connected quantum network (Alice, Bob, Chloe, and David) using time-bin encoded photons generated via type-0 spontaneous parametric down-conversion in a periodically poled lithium niobate on insulator (PPLNOI) waveguide.
key_observables: Entanglement fidelity (F), entanglement witness values (W and MDI witness I), trace-distance entanglement measure (E_Tr), and coincidence-to-accidental ratio (CAR).
important_parameters: 20-km total fiber distribution, 100 MHz repetition rate, 1126 nm SPDC bandwidth, and 5 ns temporal delay for attack simulation.
main_assumptions: The protocol assumes that the input states prepared in the polarization degree of freedom are trusted, even if the measurement devices are untrusted.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the network architecture and source schematic; Figure 3 illustrates the time-shift attack mechanism and the failure of conventional witnesses; Figure 4 details the wavelength-multiplexed distribution and the MDI verification results.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of measurement vulnerabilities, describes the PPLNOI source and network architecture, demonstrates the vulnerability of conventional witnesses to time-shift attacks, presents the MDI protocol implementation, and concludes with the experimental verification and quantification of entanglement across all network links.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it provides a practical solution for scalable, secure entanglement distribution in fiber-based networks, addressing a critical security loophole in quantum communication.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic illustration of the setups of the four-user FCQN and its characterization. (a) Conceptual schematic of the FCQN, where four users (Alice, Bob, Chloe, and David) share all six pairwise entangled links. (b) Time-bin entangled photon-pair source based on a PPLNOI waveguide. A pulsed laser is prepared in a time-bin superposition via an un- balanced Mach–Zehnder interferometer (UMZI) and drives broadband spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC). The generated photon pairs are wavelength-demultiplexed and distributed to four users via dense wavelength-division multiplex- ing (DWDM). (c) Experimental setup for conventional projective measurements, where time-bin states are...

Figure 3. Time-shift attack on entanglement witness measurement. (a) Schematic illustration of the time-shift attack. In the absence of manipulation, photons at nodes u and v produce valid coincidence events within the selected time window. Under attack, a temporal delay of ∼5 ns is introduced on the photon at node v, while the coincidence window remains fixed. As a result, coincidence events fall outside the window and are not registered, leading to biased correlation estimates. (b) Measured entanglement witness values ⟨W⟩for the separable state |euev⟩for all six user pairs. The gray and red circles denote the results obtained without and under the time-shift attack, respectively.

Figure 2. Characterization of the distributed entan- gled states for all six links in the FCQN, before and after 20-km fiber distribution. (a) Tomographic fideli- ties of the reconstructed two-photon states for each user pair. Gray (white) dots correspond to results obtained before (af- ter) fiber distribution. The dashed line at F = 0.5 indicates the classical limit [16, 29]. (b) Measured entanglement wit- ness values for the same links. The dashed line at ⟨W⟩= 0 marks the entanglement bound.

Figure 4. Results of MDI entanglement Verification and quantification for the time-bin entangled states |Φ(θ)⟩. (a) The measured MDI witness values I(ρu,v) for all six links. (b) MDI entanglement quantification for tunable time-bin states |Φ(θ)⟩in link (A, B). The green dashed curve shows the theoretical lower bound, while the purple dashed curve shows the corresponding trace-distance entanglement measure ETr(ρ), plotted on the right vertical axis. Green circles denote the experimentally measured lower bounds.
